entry.title: Understanding RISE with SAP: Simplifying the Confusion
entry.content:
This article is not for SAP experts but for individuals who are new to this area of knowledge

Nomenclature Confusion
There is confusion in the industry around the terminology, particularly with names being mixed up, such as 'S4 Rise', 'HANA Rise', 'RISE SAP', or other combinations of these terms. However, the correct term is simply 'RISE with SAP.' Now that the naming is clear.

RISE with SAP is Not a New Product Version
It’s a misconception that RISE with SAP is a new product version, like SAP R/1 (1973) the first version, R/2 (1981), R/3 (1992), ECC (2004), or S/4HANA, which was launched in 2015 and remains the latest version of SAP ERP. Product Versions were introduced with different architecture, functionality and features. The evolution of SAP's versions from R/1 to S/4HANA were for technological advancements and changing business needs over time.

Around two years ago, SAP introduced RISE with SAP. (If anyone knows the exact launch date, let me know. I’ll update the article.) But unlike a new product version release, RISE with SAP is a transformation offering. RISE with SAP can be explained as a type of transformation offering that has a bundling of existing SAP tools, assets, solutions, methodology, learning resources, etc. that focus on providing customers with business transformation-as-a-service that comes in 2 packages Premium package and Premium plus package. Probably best comparable to another offering GROW with SAP.

Without getting into what GROW with SAP exactly is, here is a table that helps explain the offering differences.

 

RISE with SAP  GROW with SAP
Targeted at large enterprises needing a full digital transformation package.Focused on small and mid-sized businesses (SMBs) for fast implementation of cloud ERP solutions.
Includes a comprehensive set of tools like ERP, SAP S/4HANA Cloud, and business process intelligence.Provides a simplified, quick-to-deploy version of SAP S/4HANA Cloud for smaller companies.
Offers flexibility for customization and integration for complex IT landscapes.Emphasizes rapid cloud adoption with pre-configured best practices.
Aimed at guiding businesses through their unique transformation journey.Designed to help smaller businesses grow efficiently using SAP's cloud solutions.
More expensive and complex, suitable for large-scale operations.More affordable and streamlined for SMBs seeking to scale quickly.

What Exactly is RISE with SAP?
RISE with SAP is designed to help clients transition their existing SAP systems to the cloud, modernize ERP usage, and increase flexibility. Its objective is to upgrade clients’ legacy ERP systems to an AI-enabled cloud ERP. It’s a (BTaaS) Business-transformation-as-a-service offering, meaning businesses receive a tailored path to modernizing their implementation and operations.

RISE with SAP: What's Included?
  • Cloud ERP (SAP S/4HANA): The core system that manages everything from finance, hr to supply chain.
  • Business Process Transformation: Tools to analyze and optimize business processes, improving efficiency.
  • Technical Migration Support: Services to simplify moving systems to the cloud.
  • Customizable Options: Adaptable features to suit different industries and company sizes.
  • Single Contract: Bundles everything under one contract for easier management and billing.

Why RISE with SAP is Important for Clients
RISE with SAP helps larger organizations move their SAP systems to the cloud by offering an all-in-one package. It simplifies the migration process, making it easier to manage without the need for separate licenses. The solution focuses on six key areas:

  • Cloud ERP
  • Business process optimization
  • Integration and automation
  • Financial management
  • Supplier network
  • Sustainability

This bundled offering reduces complexity and costs, providing a single contract and pricing for the entire transition. RISE with SAP also takes advantage of SAP’s history of simplifying workflows, ensuring that each company’s transition to the cloud is flexible and tailored to its specific needs.

In Summary
RISE with SAP is a comprehensive package offering designed to help clients move to the cloud, improve their processes, and stay competitive in the market.

I wrote this article because clients often ask me ask me about it. I aimed to keep it simple and easy to understand, even for those unfamiliar with the topic. If any enhancements or corrections are needed, feel free to message me privately, and I'll be happy to review and update it as required.

entry.title: Devtoberfest 2024 Week 4 Contest Activities
entry.content:
Please note that while we are publishing contest activities on Friday, October 18th; many badge assignments won't be active in the system until Monday, October 21st.  If you happen to complete a contest activity before the badge is active, that's OK. You'll still get credit for it. It just won't show up in your gameboard totals until the new badges are activated and assigned.

If you are looking for Week 1 activities, they can be found here: Devtoberfest 2024 Week 1 Contest Activities - SAP Community

If you are looking for Week 2 activities, they can be found here: Devtoberfest 2024 Week 2 Contest Activities - SAP Community

If you are looking for Week 3 activities, they can be found here: Devtoberfest 2024 Week 3 Contest Activities - SAP Community

Welcome to Devtoberfest 2024! We are back again this year with 4 weeks of fun and learning! Starting on September 23rd you can take part in the Devtoberfest contest (CONTEST OFFICIAL RULES HERE) and collect points for tutorials, quizzes and live sessions! Collect as many points as possible until the 25th of October and qualify for prizes such a trip to next year's SAP TechEd in person experience! For general information check out the overview about taking part in the contest here: Devtoberfest Contest - SAP Community Groups 

The table below shows you the points you can earn in the second week of Devtoberfest! Every week we will post an update with the new activities that will get you points in the contest! Each activity will earn you a badge applied to your SAP Community ID and points that you can track on the “for fun only” Devtoberfest Gameboard. The scavenger hunt is also back this year! The tutorials, quizzes and fun activities to earn even more points as part of the scavenger hunt is part of week 4. And don't forget about Fun Friday Activities.  This week's Fun Friday activity will be Spooktoberfest to celebrate Halloween. 

List of activities for contest week 4:

entry.title: SAP Community Voice: Devtoberfest, New Features, Popular Content, and More
entry.content:
Lest you think that SAP Community Voice updates have been silenced, we’re back to share the latest site updates, spotlight popular posts, and promote cool new areas for you to explore!

 

Featured Content

 

Still Time to Participate in Devtoberfest 2024!

Devtoberfest wraps up on October 25, so you still have time to participate in this event. If you’re interested in coding and collaborating – and earning badges (and maybe even prizes) for your efforts – you’ll want to join the Devtoberfest group, learn about the event-related activities, and join in!

Visit the group

 

Contest: My SAP TechEd Virtual Viewing Setup

Also ending on October 25: the “My SAP TechEd Virtual viewing setup” contest. SAP TechEd may be over, but you can still show off how you viewed the virtual event – and vote on contestants. Head on over to the SAP TechEd group to get contest details, share your entry, and view (and vote for) submissions.

Learn about the contest

 

Is Data Warehouse Required with SAP S/4HANA Embedded Analytics?

If that question grabbed your attention, you’ll want to read this blog post and check out the comments. The author (a consultant) goes through several use cases before sharing his closing remarks (and inviting feedback – of which there is plenty). See if you agree and join the conversation!

Read the blog post

 

Highlights from 2H 2024 Release of Performance and Goals Management

One of our members takes a closer look at the latest set of enhancements available in the 2H 2024 release of the SAP SuccessFactors platform. This post covers topics such as AI-assisted capabilities, improvements to goal management and continuous performance management, and delegation for performance review.

Read the blog post

 

Community News

 

Check Out the Latest SAP Community Content

Since our last SAP Community Voice newsletter, we added several new location groups: Charlotte, Madrid, and Pune. We also launched an interest group for Procurement Professionals in LAC and introduced a new discussion board for the Putting AI Ethics into Practice at SAP course. In addition, we published a new topic page dedicated to SAP Digital Currency Hub. Check out these new areas and make sure to join the groups that interest you!

 

The Latest Improvements to the SAP Community Platform

You may have already noticed these updates to the platform, but just in case…

You can now sort discussions, blog posts, and knowledge-base boards by “creation date.”

View counts have been added to blog tiles, and the date format has been changed to yyyy MMM dd. (Don’t like that format? You can switch to a style you prefer via My settings > Preferences > Display. Once there, you can modify the “Date display format” field. Just make sure to use uppercase M for the month.)

We’re expecting to roll out more improvements in a few weeks, so look for more updates later this month or in early November.

entry.title: Avoid Overengineering in Software Development: Make it simple with KISS,DRY,YAGNI and MVP
entry.content:
Overengineering in software development is like building a Swiss Army knife when all you need is a simple screwdriver. It happens when developers create overly complex solutions for problems that could have been solved more simply. While the intentions behind overengineering might be good, such as aiming for flexibility, scalability, or robustness, it often leads to unnecessary complexity, increased costs, and slower development cycles.

I’m obviously a fan of clean code principles 🙈😅. In this blog, i will highlight the overengineering practices in SAP development and introduce you to practical strategies like DRY,KISS,YANGI, and maybe MVP that will help you avoid falling into the trap of over-engineering.

 

What Is Overengineering?

Overengineering occurs when developers build solutions that are far more complicated than required for the current problem. This could mean adding extra features, implementing complex architecture, or overgeneralizing code to cater to future needs that may never arise.

some examples from our SAP life :

  • Building overly complex custom ABAP modules when SAP S/4HANA already provides standard functionality to meet the requirement.
  • Implementing complex logic within SAP Fiori/freestyle UI5 apps when a simple Fiori Elements standard list report/object list page App could deliver the required business value.

While it may seem prudent to "future-proof" the solution, this can backfire, leading to inflated development and maintenance efforts 😅.

The Hidden Cost of Overengineering

SAP implementations are often complex due to the nature of the enterprise environments they serve. SAP landscapes typically include a mix of complex integrated platforms, cloud services, and more. In such environments, developers are often tempted to create highly flexible and future-proof systems to meet all possible requirements. While this approach may seem like a safeguard against future changes, it can quickly lead to various tangible and intangible costs:

  • Increased Complexity: Complex solutions require more time to develop, test, and maintain, which can lead to budget overruns and timeline delays.
  • Reduced Agility: When a system is overly complex, it becomes harder to make changes quickly in response to new business requirements.
  • Higher Maintenance Costs: Overengineered solutions often require more effort to maintain, especially when custom code replaces standard SAP functionality.
  • Frustrated Business Stakeholders: Users may become frustrated when they see delays and costs rising while key functionalities are being deprioritized in favor of future-proofing that may never be needed.

YAGNI: You Aren't Gonna Need It

The YAGNI principle, which originated from the Extreme Programming (XP) methodology, advises developers not to implement functionality until it is absolutely necessary. It encourages a lean and focused approach, where only the most immediate and critical features are built.

In SAP projects, YAGNI can help avoid the tendency to add unnecessary complexity:

  • Avoid speculative features: SAP developers, especially when working with custom ABAP or UI5 applications, often anticipate potential future requirements. However, implementing such features too early can lead to bloated codebases. For example, designing overly generic data models or complex business logic for hypothetical use cases can complicate future enhancements.
  • Simplify development: By focusing on what is needed now, SAP teams can deliver faster and with more agility. This is particularly important in the rapidly changing enterprise environment, where business needs are often volatile. Whether it's developing custom apps using SAP Fiori or creating custom integrations in SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), YAGNI encourages the team to focus on real requirements, not assumptions.
  • Maintainability: With fewer unnecessary features, the SAP landscape remains easier to maintain and upgrade over time, reducing technical debt and improving the long-term ROI of the project.

MVP: Minimum Viable Product

MVP focuses on delivering the simplest version of a product that meets the core needs of users, allowing teams to validate their ideas quickly.

In my opinion, MVP approach is ideal for testing new ideas, especially when you have a concept you want to bring to market or when you’re operating within a budget and need a quick, straightforward solution

Example: Developing a  SAP Fiori application that is intended to manage employee requests for leave. Instead of building an extensive system with complex reporting, notifications, and user roles, the MVP could focus solely on the essential features: a simple user interface for submitting leave requests and a basic admin interface for approving them. This allows for quick feedback from users, enabling the team to prioritize additional features based on real needs rather than assumptions.

DRY: Don’t Repeat Yourself

The DRY principle in my opinion is the main approach to better code refactoring since it aligns with the clean code principles that encourage avoiding code duplication by abstracting and reusing code wherever possible. Popularized by the book, The Pragmatic Programmer, the DRY principle states that, “every piece of knowledge must have a single, unambiguous, authoritative representation within a system.” Using the principle, logic, or algorithms that have certain functionality should only appear once in an application. This also includes refraining from using the same solution or approach multiple times to solve the same problem. If there are multiple solutions to a problem, try to consolidate them into one. This leads to a cleaner and more maintainable codebase.

Example: In an SAP ABAP program, if multiple reports are generating similar output formats (e.g., customer invoices), rather than duplicating the code for formatting in each report, developers can create a single reusable function module. This modular approach reduces redundancy, simplifies future updates (since changes need to be made in only one place), and helps maintain consistency across the application.

KISS: Keep It Simple, Stupid

In software development, code refactoring is an ongoing journey! In my opinion, the ideal code is the kind that’s self-explanatory and easy to understand even for a junior developer. The KISS principle advocates for simplicity in design and implementation. Solutions should be as simple as possible without unnecessary complexity.

Example: Suppose you are developing a custom SAP UI5 application for tracking sales orders. Instead of implementing multiple complicated user interfaces with various navigation paths, a KISS approach would mean creating a straightforward layout with a single page for viewing and updating orders. This not only makes it easier for users to understand but also simplifies the development and maintenance processes.

 

Practical Tips for Avoiding Overengineering :

  • Conduct Regular Requirement Reviews: Maintain open lines of communication with stakeholders to ensure that requirements are clear and current. Regularly revisit these requirements to adapt to changes in the business landscape.
  • Prioritize Core Functionality: When designing solutions, focus on the features that provide the most value to users. Defer any non-essential features to future iterations.
  • Use Prototyping: Develop prototypes to test ideas before committing to full-scale implementation. This can help validate assumptions and reduce the risk of building unnecessary complexity.
  • Emphasize Documentation: Maintain clear and concise documentation of requirements, design decisions, and code. This can help prevent miscommunication and ensure that all team members are aligned on project goals.
  • Encourage Team Collaboration: Foster a culture of collaboration within your development team, encouraging open discussions about design choices and potential complexities. This collective wisdom can help identify and eliminate over-engineering tendencies.

Conclusion

Over-engineering is a common challenge in software development, but it can be avoided by adopting principles like DRY,KISS,YANGI and MVP , my Final word is here that, continued code refactoring is part of the game but always try to keep it as simple as possible.

“So if you want to go fast, if you want to get done quickly, if you want your code to be easy to write, make it easy to read.” ― Robert C. Martin, Clean Code: A Handbook of Agile Software Craftsmanship. The Clean Code Lesson 1 by Uncle Bob 😉   

 

Happy coding 🤗 😉!!

entry.title: Bugtoberfest Wrap Up!
entry.content:
Posting on @MichelleMoudy 's behalf.

Bugtoberfest is officially over! I hope everyone who wanted to participate was able to complete the tutorials and find all the bugs! As the week is officially over, those of us who wrote these bug hunts wanted to share the intended bugs we wrote into them so y'all had an opportunity to check your work.

By the way, while Bugtoberfest is over, Spooktoberfest is this week's Fun Friday event - check out the details in the Spooktoberfest 2024 blog post!

But before getting to that, I wanted to share a bit about the origin of the name bug used for coding errors. While most believe the origin comes from a real moth getting comfy in a relay in the Mark II at Harvard in 1947, the word actually comes from the terms bugbear or bugaboo. The term bug as used to describe an issue or error in engineering and can be seen in use by Thomas Edison in 1878, well before it was used to describe software or hardware faults.

As a fun aside, us developer advocates had a debate to the origin of the term at a dinner during TechEd Virtual and I ruined many evenings by sharing this fun fact with everyone. I even had to read aloud an article to prove that it didn't originate from the Harvard moth (which was shared as folks found it really funny a bug was caused by a real bug), as I was met with such disbelief. As a result, I was told I had to include this "fun" fact and story somewhere in relation to Bugtoberfest. 

For proof that I am in fact correct on the origin of the term bug, see here for more details and links to sources.

Now that everyone is aware of the origin of bugs, check out each of the sections below to see the bugs we purposefully wrote into our individual bug hunts. 

ABAP

Easy

 

 

 

 

CLASS ztest
 DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS test_value.
    METHODS test_value1.
  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS ztest
 IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD test_value2.
  ENDMETHOD.
  METHOD test_value1
  ENDCLASS.

 

 

 

 

Intended bugs:

Spoiler
Bug 1: At line 17, missing full stop `.` after `METHOD test_value1`.
Bug 2: At line 18, missing `ENDMETHOD` for `test_value1 METHOD`.
Bug 3: At line 15, implemented Wrong Method `test_value2` instead of `test_value`.

Medium

 

 

 

 

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Carrier View - CDS Data Model'
@Search.searchable: true
define view entity ZTest
  as selectfrom /dmo/flight as Flight
  association [1] to /DMO/I_Carrier as _Airline 
{
      @UI.lineItem: [ { position: 10, label: 'Airline'} ]
      @Search.defaultSearchElement: true
      @Search.fuzzinessThreshold: 0.7
      @ObjectModel.text.association: '_Airline'
  key Flight.carrier_id     as AirlineID,
      @UI.lineItem: [ { position: 20, label: 'Connection Number' } ]
  key Flight.connection_id  as ConnectionID,
      @UI.lineItem: [ { position: 30, label: 'Flight Date' } ]
  key Flight.flight_date    as FlightDate,
      @UI.lineItem: [ { position: 40, label: 'Price' } ]
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'
      Flight.price          as Price,
      Flight.currency_code  as CurrencyCode,
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'
      case
       when price <= 1000 or price is initial
        then cast(price as /dmo/flight_price preserving type)
       else
         cast( 1000 + (cast(price as abap.numc(16,2)) - 1000 ) * 
division(9,10,2) as /dmo/flight_price)
       end as

  /* Associations */
      _Airline
}

 

 

 

 

Intended bugs:

Spoiler
Bug 1: At line 5, `select` from written as `selectfrom`.
Bug 2: At line 6, association statement is incomplete, should have `on $projection.AirlineID = _Airline.AirlineID` after  `_Airline`.
Bug 3: At line 28, `end as` Variable Name missing and comma (,) missing after Variable name.
Bug 4: At line 26, `abap.numc` is a wrong data type, it should be `abap.dec`.

Hard

 

 

 

 

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@abapcatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZTESt1'
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: '#NOT_REQUIRED'
@EndUserText.label: 'Test'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
   serviceQuality: #X,
   sizeCategory: #S,
   dataClass: #MIXED
}
define view entity ZTest!
  with parameters
    P_Currency :/dmo/currency_code
  as select from /dmo/travel
  association [0] to /DMO/I_Overall_Status_VH_Text as _Status on $projection.Status = _Status.OverallStatus
{
  key travel_id                 as TravelId,
      description               as Description,
      total_price               as TotalPrice,
      currency_code             as CurrencyCode,
      concat_with_space(cast(total_price as abap.char( 20) ),
      $parameters.P_Curency,1) as AmountInCurrency,
      @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{ element: { name:
      '/DMO/I_Overall_Status_VH',entity: 'OverallStatus' } }]
      status                    as Status,
      _Status.Text              as OverallStatus,
}

 

 

 

 

Intended bugs:

Spoiler
Bug 1: At line 21, name of view entity should be corrected to `ZTest`.
Bug 2: At line 3, unwanted `‘ ‘` for `#NOT_REQUIRED`.
Bug 3: At line 26, unwanted `,` after `OverallStatus`. 
Bug 4: At line 15, association cardinality should be [1] instead of [0].
Bug 5: At line 23, in Syntax of `@Consumption.valueHelpDefinition` `element` instead of `entity` written.
Bug 6: At line 24, in Syntax of `@Consumption.valueHelpDefinition` `entity` instead of `element` written.
Bug 7: At line 22, in the syntax of `concat_with_space` there is a type at `$parameters.P_Curency,1)`.
Bug 8: At line 19, missing annotation for `Total_price` field.
Bug 9: At line 2, `@abapcatalog.sqlViewName` is not allowed in view entities. 

Bash

Easy

 

 

 

 

#!/usr/bin/env bash

read -p 'Enter your name: '
$answer = $REPLY
echo 'Thank you. You entered $answer.'

 

 

 

 

Intended bugs:

Spoiler
Bug 1: At line 4, in the assignment of `$REPLY` to `answer`, `answer` should not be prefixed with `$`. (this is not Perl!)
Bug 2: At line 4, in the assignment, no spaces are allowed either before or after the equals symbol `=`.
Bug 3: At line 5, inside single quotes, variables like `$answer` are not expanded (double quotes are needed here).

Medium

 

 

 

 

#!/usr/bin/env bash

set -e
set pipefail

main {

  local account="${1:?No account specified}"

  # Cause script to abend if no account data stored
  pass "btp/$account" > dev/null

  cf login
    -a "$(pass "btp/$account/cfapiendpoint")" \
    -u "$(pass "btp/$account/user")" \
    -p "$(pass "btp/$account/password")"

  echo <<EOF
Thank you.
Logged in successfully.
EOF

}

main "$@"

 

 

 

 

Intended bugs:

Spoiler
Bug 1: At line 4, `pipefail` is an option that you need to set using the `-o` option of `set`.
Bug 2: At line 6, functions can be declared with the `function` keyword like this: `function main { ... }` or without the `function` keyword (as here), but parentheses are needed before the code block, like this: `main () { ... }`.
Bug 3: At line 11, `dev/null` is relative, not absolute (i.e. there's no leading `/`) so this is not really what you want (to cleanly suppress `STDOUT`, output it must be sent to `/dev/null`).
Bug 4: At line 13, the newline at the end of `cf login` is not escaped (with a `\`) so this `cf` command execution is not valid.
Bug 5: At line 18, echo does not read from STDIN so we can't use a heredoc (<<); instead, `cat` could be used (or `printf`).

Hard

 

 

 

 

#!/usr/bin/env bash

read -p 'Enter a color: ' color

if [ $color == 'black' ]; then
    echo "Black is not a color, it is the absence of color"
fi

if [ $color =~ ^(red|green|blue)$ ]; then
    echo "You chose a primary color"
fi

cd colors
touch "$color.txt"

 

 

 

 

Intended bugs:

Spoiler
Bug 1: At line 3, using `read` without `-r` will mangle backslashes (shellcheck Informational).
Bug 2: At line 5 & 9, it's usually a very bad idea to not quote your variables, especially in a `[` construct.
Bug 3: At line 9, to use the regular expression operator `=~` you need the Bash-specific `[[ ... ]]` construct, and not the `[` command - see https://mywiki.wooledge.org/BashFAQ/031 and The open square bracket [ is an executable.
Bug 4: At line 13, not checking the success (or otherwise) of a `cd` command is dangerous, and could result in the script creating, changing or deleting files in a place you didn't expect!

jq

Easy

We want a simple list of products and the categories they're in. The output should look like this:
Chai is in the Beverages category
Chang is in the Beverages category
Aniseed Syrup is in the Condiments category
Chef Anton's Cajun Seasoning is in the Condiments category
...

The invocation of the jq script (including bugs) to produce this looks like this:

 

 

 

 

jq --raw \
  '.()|"(.ProductName) is in the \(Category.CategoryName) category" \
  products.json

 

 

 

 

and on its own, with extra whitespace, the (buggy) jq filter looks like this:

 

 

 

 

.()
| "(.ProductName) is in the \(Category.CategoryName) category"

 

 

 

 

Intended bugs:

Spoiler
Bug 1: At line 1 in the jq filter, the array iterator is `[]`, not `()` (Array Object Value Iterator).
Bug 2: At line 2 of the jq filter, the string interpolation construct is `\( ... )` so there's a backslash missing in the first instance (String Interpolation)
Bug 3: At line 2 of the jq filter, in the second instance of an expression being inserted into the string, the identity filter `.` is missing - it should be `.Category.CategoryName` (Identity)

The correct filter should be:
.[]|"\(.ProductName) is in the \(.Category.CategoryName) category"

Medium

We want to know how many products are still being sold, i.e. not discontinued. The answer should be a simple numeric value, like this (8 of the 77 total products in this data set are discontinued, so the value here is correct):
69

The invocation of the jq script (including bugs) to produce this looks like this:

 

 

 

 

jq --raw \
  'select(.Discontinued)|length' \
  products.json​

 

 

 

 

and on its own, with extra whitespace, the (buggy) jq filter looks like this:

 

 

 

 

select(.Discontinued)
| length

 

 

 

 

Intended bugs:

Spoiler
Bug 1: At line 1 of the jq filter, the outermost element in the data set is an array, so we must use map to iterate over and call `select` (Map).
Bug 2: At line 2 of the jq filter, we're after a count of the products that are not discontinued, so we need to negate the boolean expression inside the select (Select).

The correct filter should be:
map(select(.Discontinued|not))|length

Hard

Based on the data set, we want a summary of categories with the total number of units of stock for each of those categories. The output should look like this:

 

 

 

 

{
  "Beverages": 559,
  "Condiments": 507,
  "Confections": 386,
  "Dairy Products": 393,
  "Grains/Cereals": 308,
  "Meat/Poultry": 165,
  "Produce": 100,
  "Seafood": 701
}

 

 

 

 

The invocation of the jq script (including bugs) to produce this looks like this:

 

 

 

 

jq --raw \
  'group(.Category.CategoryID)|map({first|Category.CategoryName:map(.UnitsInStock)|+})|sum' \
  products.json

 

 

 

 

and on its own, with extra whitespace, the (buggy) jq filter looks like this:

 

 

 

 

group(.Category.CategoryID)
| map({
    first|Category.CategoryName:
    map(.UnitsInStock)
    | +
  })
| sum

 

 

 

 

Intended bugs:

Spoiler
Bug 1: At line 1 of the jq filter, the `group` function, which takes a path expression, is `group_by`, not `group` (Group By).
Bug 2: At line 3 of the jq filter, while `first` (which is just syntactic sugar for .[0] (Built Ins - first)) can be followed by a pipe, it can also be followed directly by the identity filter, which is missing here (should be `first.Category.CategoryName`, or `first|.Category.CategoryName` but not `first|Category.CategoryName`) (First Last Nth).
Bug 3: At line 3 of the jq filter, to use an expression as a key in a property literal, you need to wrap it in parentheses, i.e. `{(first.Category.CategoryName): ... }`, not `{first.Category.CategoryName: ...}`.
Bug 4: At line 5 of the jq filter, while `+` is valid in jq (it's an operator), it's not valid in the position it is here; the `add` filter is needed to add values in an array (Add).
Bug 5: At line 7 of the jq filter, `sum` is not a valid filter in jq, it's `add`.

The correct filter should be:
group_by(.Category.CategoryID)
| map({
    (first.Category.CategoryName):
    map(.UnitsInStock)
    | add
  })
| add

JavaScript

Easy

 

 

 

 

const bugFunc = (areThereBugs) -> {
  if (areThereBags) {
    console.log('Quick! Call an exterminator!);
  } else {
    console.log('Are you sure there aren't any bugs?');
  } else {
    console.log('No bugs here! Thank god.');
  }
}

 

 

 

 

Intended bugs:

Spoiler
Bug 1: At line 1, arrow function syntax is wrong. It should be `=>`, not `->`.
Bug 2: At line 2, variable `areThereBugs` is misspelled to `areThereBags`. 
Bug 3: At line 3, There is a missing quote at the end of the string in first console.log.
Bug 4: At line 5, The `'` in `aren't` hasn't been escaped in the second console.log.
Bug 5: At line 6, There's an extra `else` that will never be called. To fix this, line 4 should either be an `else if` or the second `else` should be removed.

Medium

 

 

 

 

for (let i = 0: i < 5: i++) {
  console.log(looped {i} times);
}

 

 

 

 

Intended bugs:

Spoiler
Bug 1: At line 1, for loop condition is using `:` instead of `;` between parameters.
Bug 2: At line 2, in the console.log statement, there are missing quotes. The correct quotes to be used are backticks `\`` for the template literal so the `i` variable is correctly updated within the string.
Bug 3: At line 2, in the console.log statement, there is a missing `$` in front of the variable curly braces `{}`.

Hard

For the last JavaScript bug hunt, "bug" identification is the name of the game. What most people call bugs or insects are actually several different classifications of the phylum arthropods.

The goal of the function in this bug hunt is to take in an object of an unidentified "bug" and determine if it is a part of the class Insecta or Arachnids, and if Insecta, further identify if it is a true bug in the order Hemiptera. To make things simple, the function should return one of the three values, "Arachnid", "Insect", or "True Bug".

Please note that some of this data might not technically be correct, entomology wise. For example, there are some arachnids with less than 8 legs. This does not count as a bug. To simplify things, the objects below are going to be used as the source of truth for arthropod classification. They should be used to determine if the function `isArachnidInsectOrBug` will return the correct output.

Arachnid

 

 

 

 

{
    antennae: false,
    wingPairs: false,
    bodySegments: 2,
    legs: 8
}

 

 

 

 

Insect

 

 

 

 

{
    antennae: true,
    wingPairs: [0, 2],
    mouthType: [
      "sponging",
      "siphoning",
      "chewing",
      "piercing/sucking flexible",
      "piercing/sucking rigid"
    ],
    bodySegments: 3,
    legs: 6
}

 

 

 

 

True Bug

 

 

 

 

{
    antennae: true,
    wingPairs: [0, 2],
    mouthType: "piercing/sucking rigid",
    bodySegments: 3,
    legs: 6
}

 

 

 

 

Hint: Keep these classification objects in mind when looking at the functions in the bug hunt.
Below are examples of potential input to the function `IsArachnidInsectOrBug`. In order to determine what is what, they need to match the values in the classification objects above. Keep in mind that all true bugs are insects but not all insects are true bugs and arachnids are neither. No coding bugs will be about the data itself being wrong.
Assume only one value in the classification object parameters with arrays will be chosen for the input object. For example, in Insect, `wingPairs` would equal either `0` or `2`, not `[0]` or `[0, 2]`. You can see this in input example 2.
Example of what could be sent into the function and expected output:
Arthropod Input Example 1

 

 

 

 

{
    antennae: false,
    wingPairs : false,
    bodySegments: 2,
    legs: 8
}

 

 

 

 

Expected Output: "Arachnid"
Arthropod Input Example 2

 

 

 

 

{
    antennae: false,
    wingPairs : false,
    mouthType: "piercing/sucking flexible",
    bodySegments: 3,
    legs: 6
}

 

 

 

 

Expected Output: "Insect"
Remember, best practices is not a justification for a bug. If the code will run and output the expected result, it does not count as a bug. Also keep in mind that the point is to count the bugs, not how to fix the bugs. One fix might take care of several bugs but should not be counted as only one bug.
These are complex bugs so look carefully.
Find the bugs in the following code:

 

 

 

 

function isArachnidInsectOrBug (arthropod) {
    let bugType = isInsectOrBug(arthropod);

    if (!bugType) {
        return isArachnid(arthropod);
    }
}

function isInsectOrBug(arthropod) {
    let bugType = "";
    switch (arthropod.mouthType) {
        case "sponging":
        case "siphoning":
        case "chewing":
        case "piercing/sucking flexible": {
            bugType = "Insect";
        }
        case "piercing/sucking rigid": {
            bugType = "True Bug";
        }
    }
}

function isArachnid ({antennae, wingPairs, bodySegments, legs}) {
    if (!antennae || !wingPairs || bodySegments === 2 || legs ===  {
        return true;
    }
}

 

 

 

 

Intended bugs:

Spoiler
Bug 1: At line 2, calling `isInsectOrBug` first will throw an error if the arthropod is an Arachnid as the arachnid object does not have the `mouthType` parameter. To fix this, call `isArachnid` first and return `Arachnid` before checking if the arthropod is an insect or true bug.
Bug 2: At line 16, in the switch statement in `isInsectOrBug`, doesn't have a break statement and will always cause `bugType` to be "True Bug".
Bug 3: At line 9, `isInsectOrBug` does not have a return statement.
Bug 4: At line 25, `isArachnid` is using the wrong boolean operators to test if arthropods is an arachnid. If any of those are correct, the function will return true. This will cause certain insects to return as Arachnid and is not correct.
Bug 5: At line 27, `isArachnid` does not return anything if bug isn't an arachnid. This could cause an error as the returned value will be null. This could be handled in the `isArachnidInsectOrBug` due to truthy values but can cause unexpected errors so returning false is the best option.
Bug 6: At line 5, `isArachnidInsectOrBug` is expecting isArachnid to return the string "Arachnid" but is instead getting a boolean. This will cause isArachnidInsectOrBug to return a boolean rather than one of the three expected strings. To fix this, `isArachnidInsectOrBug` should use the value to return either "Arachnid" or further check for insect or true bug.
Bug 7: At line 5, `isArachnidInsectOrBug` will only return if bug is an arachnid. It should return the results of `isInsectOrBug` if not an empty string and otherwise throw an error if insect is none of the three expected results.

Without bugs, this is what the function could look like (without accounting for best practices and shortening code):
function isArachnidInsectOrBug (arthropod) {
    let bugType = "";

    if (isArachnid(arthropod)) {
        bugType = "Arachnid";
    } else {
        bugType = isInsectOrBug(arthropod);
    }

    if (bugType !== "") {
        return bugType;
    } else {
        throw new Error({
            arthropod: arthropod,
            err: "Arthropod is not an Arachnid, Insect, or True Bug."
        })
    }
}

function isInsectOrBug(arthropod) {
    let bugType = "";
    switch (arthropod.mouthType) {
        case "sponging":
        case "siphoning":
        case "chewing":
        case "piercing/sucking flexible": {
            // Alternatively, the string could be returned here instead of using the break statement and the bugType variable.
            bugType = "Insect";
            break;
        }
        case "piercing/sucking rigid": {
            bugType = "True Bug";
            break;
        }
    }
    return bugType;
}

function isArachnid ({antennae, wingPairs, bodySegments, legs}) {
    // Alternatively, the result of the boolean statement in the if statement could be returned directly.
    // EX. `return (!antennae && !wingPairs && bodySegments === 2 && legs === 8);`
    if (!antennae && !wingPairs && bodySegments === 2 && legs ===  {
        return true;
    } else {
        return false;
    }
}

Python

Easy

 

 

 

 

def hello_world();
print "Hello, World!"

hello_world()

 

 

 

 

Intended bugs:

Spoiler
Bug 1: At line 1, `;` should be `:`.
Bug 2: At line 2, the print statement should be indented after the function definition on line 1.
Bug 3: At line 2, The string "Hello, World!" should be wrapped in parentheses in call to `print`.

Medium

 

 

 

 

from typing import Final

#Store integers 1, 2 and 3 in variables `ONE`, `TWO`, `THREE`
ONE, TWO, THREE = range(1, 3)
ZERO : Final = 0

def check_number (x : int) -> str:
    if x <= ONE:
        result='smaller than or equal to 1'
    elif x = TWO:
        result='equal to 2'
    then:
        result='equal to or greater than 3'

    returns result

a=input("Input the integer number: ")

print(f"{a} is {check_number(a)}")

 

 

 

 

Intended bugs:

Spoiler
Bug 1: At line 10, boolean operator '==' should be used instead of '='. 
Bug 2: At line 12, `then` should be `else`.
Bug 3: At line 15, there is an extra `s` on `returns`. It should be `return result`.
Bug 4: At line 4, `range(1, 3)` will only provide values `1` and `2`, which is not enough values for the number of variables provided. Instead, it should be `range(1, 4)`. 
Bug 5: At line 8 and 17, `<=` is not supported between instances of `str` (`a`) and `int` (`x`). To fix this, on line 17, it should be `a=int(input("Input the integer number: "))` to convert a string to an integer.

Hard

 

 

 

 

from dataclass import dataclass

class PointInTime:
    year : int
    month: int
    day  : int

point = PointInTime(1974, 12, 23)
print(point)  # Output should be: PointInTime(year=1974, month=12, day=23)

 

 

 

 

Intended bugs:

Spoiler
Bug 1: At line 1, there is no module named 'dataclass'. Instead, it should be `from dataclasses import dataclass`.
Bug 2: At line 2, before the class `PointInTime`, there needs to be a decorator, `@dataClass` as `PointInTime()` takes no arguments without the decorator.

Wrap Up

I hope everyone had fun. Let us know in the comments if you enjoyed this and want to bring it back next year. Also feel free to share bugs you found that you were proud of or bugs you struggled with. 

While Bugtoberfest might be over, Devtoberfest isn't and there's one more week of content, and another Fun Friday event left!

entry.title: CodeJam on the topic of "Getting Started with Machine Learning using SAP HANA" on October 30th
entry.content:
Hello / Cześć!

I hope you enjoyed SAP TechEd Virtual this month and are back to participating in the last week of Devtoberfest sessions 🤓

The good news: there is a chance to get face-to-face in Wrocław once again this year! We are going to have another in-person hands-on SAP CodeJam on the topic of "Getting Started with Machine Learning using SAP HANA" on October 30th: https://career.leverx.com/external-events/sap-codejam hosted by LeverX at LABO | Coworking Wrocław.

The workshop is free, but registration is required!

Best regards / Pozdrawiam serdecznie!
--Vitaliy

entry.title: Tech Day Loading…
entry.content:
Tech Day: dive into the latest innovations in SAP BTP, SAC, Datasphere and more

Two more weeks and the Tech Day will take place at the Belgian Palm Brewery on the 7th of November!

More information about the Tech Day: dive into the latest innovations in SAP BTP, SAC, Datasphere and more.

Interested in these amazing Technologies and or Beer? Do not hesitate and register now !

 

Some of the amazing sessions to be expected 🎤

Key Strategies for Effective SAP BTP Administration and Governance

Drawing from real-world experiences, we will outline best practices for establishing a robust governance framework for SAP BTP.

by @WouterLemaire & @TomCenens 

 

Five reasons to use CAP

Explore key architectural and design strengths, and look at the design time features that make CAP a pleasure to use.

by @qmacro

 

Unlocking SAP's Mobile Potential using Work Zone, Mobile Start, Fiori

by @thomasswolfs  & Tom Dhooghe 

 

Streamline processes with SAP BUILD Process Automation

We will give concrete examples of customer projects, for example streamlining master data creation, and more.

by Nico Deleener

 

Beer tasting & walking dinner 🍻

At around 5:45 PM we'll call it a day, grab a drink (you can even participate in a beer tasting) and a bite and start connecting.

 

We hope to see you there!

entry.title: Unofficial SAP Community Feedback Page
entry.content:
How ironic that I couldn't find a place to categorise this post.
So now it is simply in the Technology section with the title 'SAP Community'.
There is definitely enough feedback and suggestions for improvement for this SAP Community website.
At the moment a lot of people have given feedback, but it is scattered and it makes sense to collect and prioritise the points.
Not only for SAP, but also for the community to be able to always refer to a central point.

That is why I have created an instance of the open source software fider, which is available under the domain https://fider.marianzeis.de
Here you can create and comment on proposals, and everyone can vote on proposals.

You can always log in with your email and don't need a password.
It may happen that the login email ends up in the spam folder.

What do you think of this idea?

entry.title: The Roadmap to Success: Leveraging Best Practices and Activate Methodology for S/4HANA Implementatio
entry.content:
Navigating the path to a successful S/4HANA implementation may be daunting, but by leveraging best practices and SAP’s Activate methodology, businesses can streamline the process and gain the most value from their investment. In this post, we’ll explore how to make the most of scope items, ready-to-run business processes, documentation, and tools like the Signavio Process Navigator.

Understanding the SAP Activate Methodology

The SAP Activate methodology is a unique, modular, and agile framework that guides you through the transformation process. It encompasses four stages: Prepare, Explore, Realize, and Deploy. The methodology offers a roadmap, providing businesses with step-by-step guidance, accelerators, and best practices to achieve a smooth implementation.

 

 

In this blogpost we will mainly focus on the power of the best practices.

The Power of Best Practices

 

In the S/4HANA implementation journey, SAP’s best practices are crucial. These pre-packaged, ready-to-run business processes encapsulate the collective wisdom of SAP experts acquired from a myriad of successful projects. Each is detailed in scope items, each representing a unique business scenario replete with associated business processes and configuration settings.

These best practices come with comprehensive documentation, such as process diagrams, configuration guides, and data loading procedures, providing your team with a wealth of invaluable information to leverage. By using these best practices, you can speed up your S/4HANA implementation, reduce risks, and ensure alignment with tested strategies.

In the continuous spirit of innovation and to further enhance user experience, SAP has introduced the new Process Navigator, designed to replace the Best Practice Explorer. This cutting-edge tool promises to revolutionize how businesses access and utilize best practices.

The Process Navigator provides a more streamlined, intuitive, and interactive experience, allowing users to navigate through various processes and scope items in a more efficient and organized manner. It offers greater visibility into processes, helping users understand the context, interactions, and the flow of business processes better.

It also enhances the way you can search for and access documentation related to different scope items and processes. This ensures a more integrated, holistic view of best practices, promoting a more efficient and effective S/4HANA implementation process.

 

As you transition to using the new Process Navigator, remember that it’s designed with the user in mind. It combines the powerful functionalities of the Best Practice Explorer with a more user-friendly interface and additional capabilities, promising a more seamless experience in leveraging best practices for your S/4HANA implementation.

 

Making the Most of Scope Items and Leveraging the Solution Builder

Scope items are pivotal to unlocking the power of SAP’s best practices. Each scope item is a ready-to-use business process that can be activated straight away, with relevant software requirements, implementation efforts, necessary data, and configuration guidelines detailed within.

A key tool to fully utilize scope items is the SAP Solution Builder. This robust tool is instrumental in aiding organizations to create a structured and tailored implementation guide based on SAP’s best practices. It allows the selection of the required scope items and generates a pre-configured solution that caters to your business requirements.

Moreover, Solution Builder allows organizations to customize scope items and add new ones, offering flexibility in shaping the solution to meet specific business needs. This capability ensures that organizations can adjust the pre-configured solutions to match their processes, leading to increased efficiency and productivity.

 

One of the important steps in making the most of scope items and the Solution Builder is to understand your business needs fully. By comprehending your business’s requirements, processes, and needs, you can identify the most relevant scope items for your organization. This understanding will then guide your mapping of the implementation plan, backed by SAP’s extensive experience and best practices.

Lastly, remember that there are comprehensive step-by-step guides available online to aid you in leveraging the Solution Builder and implementing scope items. These guides provide in-depth explanations, screenshots, and practical tips to assist your team during the implementation process. These resources can be instrumental in avoiding common pitfalls and ensuring a smooth and successful implementation of S/4HANA. Here is a link to the awsome blog series from Mahesh Sardesai: New Installation of SAP S/4HANA 2022 FPS1 – Part 3 – Best Practices Content Activation | SAP Blogs. I’m folowwing his step by step guides eversince they started publishing it.

 

Documentation: The Backbone of your Implementation

Thorough and accurate documentation is key to ensuring a smooth and successful S/4HANA implementation. Documentation within scope items provides detailed insights into the steps required for implementing each business process, configuration guides for each scenario, test scripts for validation, and procedures for data loading.

Maintaining an updated and comprehensive documentation set will help your implementation team understand the processes and guide them through each stage. It also provides a reference for future troubleshooting and audits, allowing for continuous improvement and upkeep.

 

 

 

Streamlining your Implementation with Signavio Process Navigator

One of the challenges during an S/4HANA implementation is managing and understanding the multitude of business processes and their interactions. This is where the Signavio Process Navigator comes in. This powerful tool allows you to map, manage, and optimize your business processes, providing a clear visual representation of your process landscape.

It facilitates an end-to-end perspective of your processes, helping to identify potential bottlenecks, inefficiencies, or areas that require improvement. Moreover, the tool can simulate various scenarios, enabling you to test and refine your processes before going live. By incorporating the Signavio Process Navigator into your implementation plan, you can ensure that your S/4HANA implementation aligns perfectly with your business operations and objectives.

 

Conclusion

Leveraging SAP’s best practices and the Activate methodology, coupled with a clear understanding of scope items, comprehensive documentation, and tools like the Signavio Process Navigator, can significantly streamline your S/4HANA implementation. This approach not only mitigates risks but also ensures that your organization reaps the full benefits of your S/4HANA. Providing you a jumpstart of your S/4 Journey.

SAP Signavio Process Navigator SAP Signavio Process Navigator

entry.title: Coach's Corner: The Essential Act of Receiving
entry.content:
When organizations start out on the path of creating a “Feedback Culture,” they are so focused on the delivery methodology that they forget the important other-half of the equation – receiving.

 

Give. Give. Give.

It’s no wonder. In the West especially, we are instructed by faith leaders to “Give, and you will receive.” Since we were young, we were taught by parents and teachers to share, because “sharing means caring.” In school, we were expected to give our opinions, ask questions and share ideas as part of the learning process. We are encouraged to give compliments because this will result in making friends and keeping neighbours happy. In life, this ‘Pay It Forward’ mindset spawns an entire industry of charities and crowd-funding outlets for doing-good. Finally, at work, we are expected to join training programs and learn how to give feedback to our colleagues, our managers, our suppliers and even our customers.

 

To receive.

By contrast, in Eastern societies, children are taught to receive. I mean receiving in the way of accepting, and want to distinguish this from ‘taking.’ The main role of children in the East is to receive the resources, opportunities and energy of their parents and elders, and to focus on studying and preparing for the future. From a very young age, they were taught to listen in class and receive the knowledge of their teachers. When it comes to work and life, the practice of receiving shows up as acceptance and common understanding of each person’s role in a team and in an organization. The Taoist tenet to “be like water” shapes the values, and therefore the mindset and behaviours of many in Eastern society.

 

We seem to have an easier time giving feedback, advice and opinions, yet struggle with receiving them when they are offered to us. See my post “Feedback: The Gift That’s Hard to Accept” for reasons why we struggle with this. One action is not better than the other. However, without the corresponding practice of receiving there would be no value in the feedback. By receiving and accepting someone’s gift, we also honour them and fulfill their good intentions. The combination of both giving and receiving completes the equation.  

 

How do we become better at receiving?

  • Be present – Preparing our mind, our breath, and our emotions isn’t just reserved for delivering presentations or giving bad news. We must also prepare ourselves to receive, by calming our mind and focusing on the person we’re with. If you’re not in a state to receive, then you’re not going to get the maximum benefit of the gift.

  • Assume best intentions – Our biases and interpretations stand in the way of learning. Focus on the content and the words to remove biases of who may be delivering the message. This also means keeping your ego in check – even the most junior member of your team or the most unexpected source can offer you value.

  • Be curious and ask questions – Some of us need to overcome our default to only give, while others may need to strengthen our propensity to receive. Our ability to receive can be learned and reinforced by asking simple questions. For example, “Would you be OK if I asked for some more feedback in the future?” 

 

In our life, there will be times when we see things as opposing each other – ebb and flow; push and pull; Yin and Yang; workers and management. When we choose to look at these forces as part of the same system, they are actually complimentary. In developing a Culture of Feedback, we need to be as good at receiving as we are at giving.

 

[Reposted from the Stone Soup Coaching blog]

entry.title: A Historic Milestone: SAP Inside Track Pune 2024
entry.content:
The first-ever SAP Inside Track (SIT) Pune 2024 was a resounding success, and We couldn’t be more thrilled to share the recap of this incredible event. After years of anticipation and hard work, it was a dream come true to host this milestone event in our city—an event that not only showcased Pune’s dynamic SAP community but also set the stage for bigger things to come.

 

We saw over 200+ participants, 20+ distinguished speakers, and 20+ volunteers come together to make this a truly unforgettable experience.

 

### A Grand Kickoff

The day kicked off with inspirational keynotes that set the tone for the event. We were honored to have from Sindhu Gangadharan, MD of SAP Labs India and Head of Customer Innovation Services, SAP, who delivered a vibrant virtual keynote. We also had the privilege of hearing Saicharan Krishnamoorthy (@saicharank) , VP of Database Engineering at SAP and SAP Labs Pune Site Lead, take the stage with a powerful keynote that energized the audience, bringing invaluable insights to our attendees.

Adding to the list of inspiring speakers, we were also joined by Sambhaji Chawale, CEO of Primus Techsystems Private Limited, our esteemed partner for SIT Pune 2024. His words were not only motivational but also highlighted the importance of collaboration within the SAP ecosystem.

The main stage was beautifully hosted by Punith Oswal (@Punith_Oswal), SAP Champion and member of the SIT core team. His energy and expertise set the perfect tone for the day’s sessions.

 

### Sessions and Tracks

The event featured a variety of sessions divided across four different tracks, allowing attendees to dive deep into the topics that piqued their interest. Each session was packed with invaluable insights and the latest trends in the SAP ecosystem:

Our Speakers:

Attendees had the flexibility to choose the sessions that interested them the most, making it a highly engaging and personalized experience for all.

 

### Gratitude and Collaborations

This event wouldn’t have been possible without the incredible support of our partners, speakers, and volunteers. A heartfelt thank you to Primus Tech Systems, Deloitte, Mindset, and SAP Press —your collaboration was instrumental in making this event such a success.

 

A special shoutout goes to Saicharan Krishnamoorthy (@saicharank ) and Devprasad Kambli (@Devkambli ) for their unwavering support and guidance throughout this journey. Your dedication ensured everything ran smoothly, and for that, we are truly grateful.

A special thank you to Pushpendra Yadav, Kishor Ingale, and Ranjeet Kumar for their invaluable assistance and support throughout the event.

Of course, a massive thank you to all our attendees! Your enthusiasm and energy brought this event to life. The first-ever SIT in Pune would not have been the same without your passion and dedication. The event was a true testament to Pune’s vibrant and growing SAP community.

Our Support System – The SIT Core Team and the Volunteers

### Memorable Moments and Key Takeaways

One of the key highlights of the event was witnessing the enthusiasm of Pune’s tech community. Seeing professionals and learners from all over India come together to share knowledge, network, and explore new ideas in the SAP world was incredibly inspiring. Many participants travelled from different cities, which added to the event’s diversity and spirit.

The event also featured networking opportunities, where participants connected with peers, SAP experts, and thought leaders. These discussions provided a platform for building long-term collaborations, learning about new innovations, and enhancing skills in various aspects of SAP technology and integration.

The day was not just about sessions and keynotes but about building relationships and strengthening the sense of community. From participants to partners, everyone walked away with valuable insights, and We believe we all contributed to something truly special in Pune’s SAP ecosystem.

### Looking Ahead

As We reflect on the success of SIT Pune 2024, We can’t help but feel excited about what’s to come. We have put Pune firmly on the SAP map, and this is just the beginning. The energy and passion displayed at this event have laid the foundation for even greater opportunities and collaboration in the future.

 

Let’s keep the momentum going! If you missed out on this event, make sure to follow our SAP Inside Track Pune pages on LinkedIn and the SAP Pune Community group for all the latest updates, future events, and content. The future of SAP in Pune is bright, and we’re just getting started! 🚀

Thank you once again to everyone who contributed to making this event a tremendous success. Here’s to many more milestones for the SAP community in Pune and beyond

 

Cheers,

SAP Community Pune Core Team

entry.title: TechEd On Tour in our very own Melbourne
entry.content:
It’s a wrap! Yes, sadly, SAP TechEd Virtual is already over, but it’s been a blast! But the good news is: If you want to continue to learn, network, and bring out your best, you can still participate in one of five SAP TechEd on Tour or SAPinsider events worldwide – with learning sessions and meet-ups with SAP experts. Here’s what you need to know to come and say “Hi”: 

SAP is offering five separate tour stops around the globe, organized in collaboration with SAP user groups. Each stop will offer hands-on learning opportunities, deep-dive lecture sessions, and meet-the-experts all presented by SAP experts, partners, and many customers. We are also bringing back the Developer Garage for you as a great opportunity to learn by doing. 

Choose the US or Europe to meet SAP experts 

From November 12-14, join technical practitioners and SAP experts at ASUG Tech Connect 2024 in West Palm Beach for immersive sessions, SAP insights, and networking. Over three days, you'll enhance your skills, stay at the forefront of digital advancements, and grow professionally. 

If you prefer a trip to Europe, SAPinsider EMEA 2024 in Copenhagen, Denmark, is for you. From November 12-14, you can develop the skills and expertise needed to thrive in your role. Hear from customers about their successful digital transformations, learn implementation best practices from SAP partners, and connect with peers and SAP experts in a three-day learning event aligned with SAP TechEd Virtual.  

Get an overview of the SAP ecosystem in Australia 

If you fancy a trip to Down Under, we’re excited to meet you at SAUG-x 2024 this year! From November 19-20 you can learn from unique insights, network with your peers, hear from SAP experts, and get an overview of our ecosystem. Don’t miss these relevant knowledge sharing, learning, and networking sessions – all in Melbourne, Australia! 

And if you’re still in the mood for SAP TechEd by December, join us in Birmingham, UK, at UKISUG Connect 2024. At the ICC in Birmingham, expect to learn from experts, network with like-minded peers and collaborate together. Benefit from knowledge-rich keynotes, engaging breakout sessions, a range of workshops and an exhibition hall of over 70 SAP partners. 

Last, but not least, head over to the motherland of SAP – Germany – and join us at DSAG-TechXchange 2025 on April 4, 2025! Yes, it might be a couple of months away, but having your next trip planned is always a nice thing, don’t you think? 

Whichever location you prefer – we are looking forward to seeing you there! Make use of the unique opportunity to connect with like-minded individuals, gain valuable insights into the tech industry, and get hands-on experience with the latest SAP technologies. Whether you're a developer, architect, IT manager, or business executive, SAP TechEd on Tour has something for everyone. Don't miss out on this opportunity to stay ahead of the curve and propel your tech career to new heights! 

For more information on the SAP TechEd on Tour stops, visit this page. 

And while you’re at it, make sure to attend SAP CodeJam events in your area - free, hands-on coding experiences where you can collaborate with fellow developers to solve real-world challenges while diving deep into the latest SAP technologies.

entry.title: Next SAP Stammtisch St.Gallen coming soon
Evento SAP x Team Liquid
entry.content:
Em setembro desse ano, a nossa universidade foi convidada à participar de um evento da SAP em colaboração com a Team Liquid, onde alguns alunos selecionados puderam ter uma experiência completa, imersiva e muito educativa que impressionou todos os presentes. Procuramos convidar pessoas que pertenciam à Liga de Pesquisa, Jogos e E-sports oficial da UPM, cujos perfis e interesses se enquadravam melhor ao evento proposto, e não poderia ter sido melhor. 

As expectativas de todos estavam um pouco baixas, pois quando pensamos em SAP normalmente nos remete a algo extremamente corporativo, um pouco monótono, e principalmente considerando que o primeiro dia seria uma palestra, acredito que o pessoal estava um pouco desanimado - e já adianto que todos ficaram muito mais do que satisfeitos.

Assim que chegamos ao escritório, fomos recebidos pelos incríveis Luca Cherubim Ferracuti e Isabella Aimola, que nos acomodaram e introduziram um pouco do ambiente da empresa, seus cargos e nos contaram um pouco como seria nosso dia no escritório. Eu e meus colegas nos deliciamos com as histórias e o catering maravilhoso que nos foi servido, nos acomodando para assistir à palestra pouco tempo depois. Mesmo para mim, que não sou da área de programação ou sistemas, foi algo extremamente didático, não me senti perdida no assunto e consegui aproveitar tanto quanto meus colegas que são da área. Pude ver que todos ficaram impressionados com o que nos foi apresentado sobre a plataforma, o que aguçou o interesse da maioria que, ao final da palestra, não paravam de comentar sobre o quão impressionados estavam por ter sido algo tão interessante. Finalizando o dia, fizemos um tour pelas instalações e pudemos também ter um momento de networking - uma sucessão e conjunto de acontecimentos que acredito que mudou completamente a visão dos meus colegas em relação à SAP.

Uma semana depois chegou o dia da tão esperada visita ao escritório da Team Liquid aqui em São Paulo, e não poderia estar mais grata à SAP por essa oportunidade. Todos chegamos cedo a local, onde visitaríamos as instalações e participaríamos de outra palestra com a equipe da Team Liquid e com dois membros da equipe de e-Sports da SAP, Thomas Esser e Joshua Goetz, que nos mostraram como funciona a plataforma de análise de dados feita exclusivamente para as equipes de League of Legends (LOL) e Dota da Liquid. Conhecemos também a psicóloga e treinadora Natália Zakalski, que nos mostrou diversas atividades e treinamentos realizados pelos atletas profissionais que carregam o nome da marca. Finalizamos o dia conhecendo o streamer Leaonardo "frttt" Braz e o time profissional feminino de Valorant, ganhamos alguns brindes maravilhosos de ambas as empresas e ainda desfrutamos de um almoço com toda a equipe. 

Acredito que, com esse evento, meus colegas ficaram ainda mais interessados e maravilhados com as possibilidades que o SAP proporciona, e pudemos ver como podemos atrelar nossos hobbies, gostos e interesses pessoais com nosso ramo de atuação. Tenho certeza absoluta que falo por todos quando digo que foi uma experiência de crescimento profissional e networking incrível, poder conhecer tantas pessoas que trabalham com o que nós sonhamos um dia trabalhar, ver de perto as instalações de duas empresas tão grandes, gigantes nos mercados em que atuam, foi algo realmente surreal e espero que seja o primeiro evento de muitos outros que possamos participar.

Muito obrigada à toda equipe organizadora por toda atenção, carinho e dedicação em fazer tudo isso acontecer e pela oportunidade incrível que proporcionou à mim e aos meus colegas! Um agradecimento especial também ao Luca Ferracuti e à Gloria Sandi, da equipe de University Alliances, e aos professores Fabio Kazuo Ohashi e Alberto Medeiros, da Universidade Presbiteriana Mackenzie, que foram os responsáveis por fazer essa conexão. Tenho certeza de que nunca vou me esquecer dessa experiência, espero poder viver tudo isso novamente em breve! Até breve SAP!

entry.title: SAP Fieldglass - New Onboarding Resource Center
entry.content:
Our ISBN Customer Onboarding Team has revamped our Onboarding Resource Centers to include new content, a more organized structure, and cleaner look. The new ORC contains content fit for each stage of a customer's journey highlighted below:

Gain an understanding of the main SAP channels and how to get access to the your solutions.

Plan Your Implementation

Learn how to run your solutions leveraging SAP tools and best practices.

Get Enabled

Increase your knowledge and skills with SAP enablement resources to effectively use your solution.

Prepare for Go Live

Find resources to seamlessly onboard your team and kickstart projects on the path to go live: New SAP Fieldglass Onboarding Resource Center

entry.title: SAP Inside Track Fortaleza 2024: Uma Celebração da Comunidade SAP no Nordeste
entry.content:
O SAP Inside Track Fortaleza em 2024 mostrou que a comunidade SAP no Brasil está mais vibrante do que nunca. Este ano, o evento bateu seu recorde de público, reunindo mais de 200 participantes em um espaço que ficou pequeno diante de tanta energia e entusiasmo. A comunidade SAP de Fortaleza e do Nordeste, que desde 2020 participa desses encontros voltados para aprendizado, networking e trocas de experiências, agora já pede um local maior para comportar o sucesso crescente do evento.

 Uma Semana de Conhecimento e Networking

Seguindo a tradição, o evento começou uma semana antes, com uma série de apresentações virtuais ao vivo. Esse Track online trouxe conteúdos em português e inglês, ampliando o acesso ao conhecimento para a comunidade. Quem quiser conferir esses conteúdos pode acessar todos os vídeos no canal oficial, onde estão disponíveis gratuitamente.

Durante o evento presencial, uma transmissão ao vivo pelo YouTube permitiu que pessoas de todo o mundo acompanhassem as palestras e interações do SAP Inside Track Fortaleza em tempo real. A transmissão foi dividida em duas partes (link da 1 parte e link da 2 parte) e está disponível no canal do evento, permitindo que mesmo aqueles que não puderam estar presentes no local tenham acesso a todo o conteúdo e experiências do dia.

Participar do SAP Inside Track ao vivo é sempre especial. No evento presencial, os participantes têm a chance de encontrar colegas com quem só interagem virtualmente e reencontrar amigos de projetos passados. Esse ambiente único promove um networking valioso e cria oportunidades para fortalecer laços profissionais e pessoais.

Além das conexões, o evento também oferece uma agenda rica de palestras ao vivo. O público teve a oportunidade de interagir diretamente com os palestrantes, fazendo perguntas e trocando ideias sobre os temas discutidos. Esse formato proporciona um aprendizado dinâmico e enriquece a experiência de todos.

A programação trouxe temas variados e palestrantes de peso. Confira abaixo alguns dos destaques:

  • MakeValue - Fátima e Gislâine - Projeto de Automação da Leitura de Extratos e Conciliação Bancária
  • Maycon Franco - Potencialidades da AI na migração S/4 HANA
  • Anselmo Milani e Bruno Lopes - Inovação Responsável:Integrando ESG na Estratégia de negócios com SAP
  • Igor Favacho - Stefanini - Cocriando soluções para um futuro melhor
  • Edson Thomaz - SAP Business Transformation Management
  • Marssel Vilaça - Compreendendo Valores Planejados na Contabilidade de Custos no SAP S/4HANA
  • Plauto Benevides e João Otto – Case de sucesso na Implementação S/4HANA M. Dias Branco
  • Lucas Ferreira - Tachyonix - Clean Core–O que significa? Como faz?
  • Sponsor CRiT - Roberta Vasconcelos e Renato
  • Jonys Arcanjo - IA Generativa e O futuro do Trabalho
  • Jefferson Alcântara - SAP Transportation Management - Overview
  • Luciano Casado - Using API with S/4HANA Public Cloud

Encerramento em Grande Estilo

Para encerrar o evento em alto astral, os participantes foram surpreendidos com uma apresentação de forró pé de serra, um dos ritmos mais autênticos da cultura nordestina. Foi um momento de celebração e descontração, onde todos puderam se divertir e comemorar o sucesso do evento.

Encerramento SAP Inside Track Fortaleza 2024

 O SAP Inside Track Fortaleza 2024 foi um verdadeiro sucesso, graças à dedicação e competência da equipe organizadora. Agora, a expectativa está ainda maior para as próximas edições, já que a comunidade SAP do Nordeste continua crescendo e mostrando cada vez mais interesse em fazer parte desse encontro tão especial.

A todos os organizadores, palestrantes, participantes e Patrocinadores, nosso muito obrigado! Este evento é uma prova da força da comunidade SAP no Brasil e da importância de espaços como o SIT Fortaleza para o crescimento e fortalecimento de nossos profissionais.

Links Importantes:

SIT Fortaleza

https://www.linkedin.com/groups/12467481/

Fortaleza - SAP Community

https://www.youtube.com/@sitfor-sapinsidetrackforta6071

Fotos: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1NEG0Lu3LnLO-5cDHwvunCfKuoq3abi6w?usp=sharing

entry.title: SAP CodeJam HANA ML in Wrocław 2024-10 Recap
entry.content:
At the end of October, we had the Getting Started with Machine Learning using SAP HANA and Python in Wrocław, Poland, kindly hosted by LeverX and organized by @Olena_Nadtochii. 

We had a fantastic group of participants, ranging from seasoned SAP veterans...

...to fresh talents who recently graduated from university.

This SAP CodeJam took place in a historical building—the former seat of the Faculty of Pharmacy of Wrocław University: a part of the university buildings' frontage, stretching along the Odra River in the northern part of the Old Town. The view from the top was breathtaking: from the Gothic Cathedral on the right to Concordia Design on the left—the Building of the Year 2021, according to ArchDaily.

And the food was equally fabulous.

I’d like to thank all the participants who joined me for this final SAP CodeJam of 2024!

The 2024 tour included 14 stops for me, and now it has come to an end.

Now, the next stops are in 2025, and I hope to see you in Spain ( @ajmaradiaga ), UAE ( @athavanraja ), India ( @Shilpa_Shankar ). Would you like to host such an SAP CodeJam in your location? Here are the details: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/quot-getting-started-with-machine-learning-usin...
